questions.stem: A patient develops this widespread skin sloughing after starting a sulfonamide. Which severe cutaneous adverse reaction does this represent?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Stevens-Johnson syndrome / toxic epidermal necrolysis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Pemphigus vulgaris

choices.label: C
choices.text: Erythema multiforme minor

choices.label: D
choices.text: Staphylococcal scalded skin syndrome

choices.label: E
choices.text: Bullous pemphigoid

explanation.summary: The photograph shows large-scale epidermal desquamation, the hallmark of Stevens-Johnson syndrome and its more extensive form toxic epidermal necrolysis. These are rare but potentially fatal hypersensitivity reactions to sulfonamides that mandate immediate discontinuation of the drug.
explanation.whyCorrect: The photograph shows large-scale epidermal desquamation, the hallmark of Stevens-Johnson syndrome and its more extensive form toxic epidermal necrolysis. These are rare but potentially fatal hypersensitivity reactions to sulfonamides that mandate immediate discontinuation of the drug.

questions.stem: The functional group depicted here forms the shared active core of which class of antibiotics?

choices.label: A
choices.text: Tetracyclines

choices.label: B
choices.text: Aminoglycosides

choices.label: C
choices.text: Sulfonamides

choices.label: D
choices.text: Fluoroquinolones

choices.label: E
choices.text: Beta-lactams

explanation.summary: The structure shows a central sulfur double-bonded to two oxygens and single-bonded to a nitrogen (the sulfonamide moiety) with variable R groups. This sulfonamide functional group defines the sulfa drug class, which acts as competitive inhibitors of bacterial folate synthesis.
explanation.whyCorrect: The structure shows a central sulfur double-bonded to two oxygens and single-bonded to a nitrogen (the sulfonamide moiety) with variable R groups. This sulfonamide functional group defines the sulfa drug class, which acts as competitive inhibitors of bacterial folate synthesis.

questions.stem: This diagram shows where sulfonamides and trimethoprim act within a bacterial cell. What is the basis for their selective toxicity toward bacteria?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The drug is only activated by bacterial beta-lactamase

choices.label: B
choices.text: Human cells rapidly efflux the drug via P-glycoprotein

choices.label: C
choices.text: Human cells cannot synthesize folate and instead import it from the diet, so they lack the targeted enzyme

choices.label: D
choices.text: Bacterial ribosomes bind the drug but human ribosomes do not

choices.label: E
choices.text: Sulfonamides cannot cross the human cell membrane

explanation.summary: The schematic shows PABA being converted through DHF to THF inside the bacterium, with sulfonamides blocking the folate-synthesis step. Mammalian cells lack dihydropteroate synthase and obtain preformed folate from the diet, so blocking de novo folate synthesis harms bacteria selectively.
explanation.whyCorrect: The schematic shows PABA being converted through DHF to THF inside the bacterium, with sulfonamides blocking the folate-synthesis step. Mammalian cells lack dihydropteroate synthase and obtain preformed folate from the diet, so blocking de novo folate synthesis harms bacteria selectively.

questions.stem: Based on the two enzyme-blocking points shown in this pathway, why does pairing a sulfonamide with trimethoprim produce a synergistic, bactericidal effect?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The combination inhibits bacterial DNA gyrase in addition to folate synthesis

choices.label: B
choices.text: Trimethoprim converts the sulfonamide into an active bactericidal metabolite

choices.label: C
choices.text: The two drugs block sequential steps of the same folate pathway, compounding the reduction in tetrahydrofolate

choices.label: D
choices.text: Trimethoprim prevents efflux of the sulfonamide from the cell

choices.label: E
choices.text: Both drugs bind the 30S ribosomal subunit to halt protein synthesis

explanation.summary: The flowchart shows sulfonamides inhibiting dihydropteroate synthase and trimethoprim/pyrimethamine inhibiting dihydrofolate reductase, two consecutive steps leading to tetrahydrofolic acid. Blocking both steps sharply reduces THF for nucleotide and protein synthesis, converting two bacteriostatic agents into a bactericidal combination.
explanation.whyCorrect: The flowchart shows sulfonamides inhibiting dihydropteroate synthase and trimethoprim/pyrimethamine inhibiting dihydrofolate reductase, two consecutive steps leading to tetrahydrofolic acid. Blocking both steps sharply reduces THF for nucleotide and protein synthesis, converting two bacteriostatic agents into a bactericidal combination.
